Khaana Ho

खाना हो तो आना
Siddhi Pednekar
📷
Tap to add Siddhi's photo

Siddhi Pednekar is the person behind Khaana Ho To Aana — a food project about the quiet, unremarkable meals that hold everything together. Not restaurant food. Not food for the internet. Food that is made because someone is hungry, or cold, or just home.

She documents the recipes, the stories, and the habits of Indian home cooking — for herself and for anyone who has ever felt far from a kitchen that felt like theirs.

Follow @khaana.ho.to.aana
Ghar ka Glass

A small digital recipe book for the drinks that Indian homes have always made — without a recipe card, with whatever is in the kitchen. Chaas churned in a blue matka. Nimbu pani squeezed by hand. Rasam simmering on a winter morning. Each one is a memory before it is a recipe.

More drinks are added season by season — twelve drinks, twelve months, one kitchen.

What you will pick up

Every recipe in this collection teaches you something specific. Not just what to make, but how to think while making it.

Chaas · Nimbu Pani · Shikanji
Balancing flavour — how sweet, sour, salt and heat hold each other in place.
Rasam
Tempering (tadka) — why you bloom spices in fat, and what it does to a dish.
Adrak Chai · Cutting Chai
Infusion and concentration — how time, heat and ratio change the same base ingredients entirely.

Throughout each recipe, some words are underlined like this. Tap them to read what the ingredient or technique means — and what you can use instead if it is unfamiliar.

twelve drinks · twelve months · one kitchen

Buttermilk · Spiced · Savory
Chaas
छाछ
Churned curd with green chilli, ginger, cumin and coriander — poured over ice and froth. The drink that makes Indian summers survivable.
☀️
Best in March – JunePeak summer. Drink cold, drink often. Cools the body from the inside out.
Beginner
You are learning emulsification by churning — how mechanical agitation creates a creamy, frothy texture from simple ingredients.
10
min prep
0
cooking
80
kcal/glass
Serves
2
glasses
Ingredients
Method
1
Add curd to a tall vessel or blender. Whisk vigorously until completely smooth — no lumps.
2
Crush green chilli and ginger together in a mortar. Aim for a rough paste, not a liquid.
✦ Rough texture releases oils differently than blending. Don't skip this.
3
Add the paste, cold water, salt, cumin powder and chaat masala to the curd. Stir well.
4
Churn with a mathani in brisk back-and-forth strokes for 2–3 minutes until the surface turns frothy.
✦ No mathani? Blend 30 seconds on medium — the froth is what makes Chaas, Chaas.
5
Pour over ice into tall glasses. Finish with coriander leaves and a pinch of chilli powder on the froth.
✦ Nani's Notes
The colder the curd, the better the froth. Set it in the fridge overnight and churn straight from cold. A tiny pinch of hing (asafoetida) if digestion is your goal — it makes all the difference.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸
#GharKaGlass  #KhaanaHoToAana
Lemon · Sweet-Salt · Refreshing
Nimbu Pani
नींबू पानी
India's original lemonade — tart fresh lime balanced with black salt and a touch of sweetness. Simple, perfect, irreplaceable on a hot afternoon.
☀️
Best in April – AugustFrom the first hot days through the monsoon — the one drink that never fails.
Beginner
You are learning flavour balancing — adjusting sweet, sour, salt and mineral until each element holds the others in place.
5
min prep
0
cooking
45
kcal/glass
Serves
2
glasses
Ingredients
Method
1
Squeeze the limes into a large jug. Remove seeds but keep the pulp — it adds body.
2
Add sugar and stir until fully dissolved. For a cleaner result, dissolve in 2 tbsp warm water first.
✦ Warm water dissolves sugar instantly — no gritty texture in the final drink.
3
Add kala namak (black salt). It gives that unmistakable mineral depth — don't skip it.
4
Pour in cold water and stir well. Taste and adjust — it should sit sharply between sweet and sour with a salty finish.
✦ Trust your taste here. The balance is everything.
5
Fill glasses with ice, pour, and garnish with a lime wheel, mint sprig and a pinch of roasted cumin.
✦ Nani's Notes
Always freshly squeezed — never bottled. And kala namak is non-negotiable: that sulphuric, mineral note is what separates Indian nimbu pani from every other lemonade in the world. A few fresh mint leaves if you have them.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸

Tamarind · Peppery · South Indian
Rasam
रसम
South India's ancient healing broth — tangy tamarind, fiery black pepper, fragrant curry leaves and a mustard tempering. Drunk straight from a cup or poured over rice. Restorative in every sense.
🌧️
Best in October – FebruaryMonsoon and winter. Drink hot, drink when unwell, drink just because.
Intermediate
You are learning tempering (tadka) — blooming whole spices in hot fat to release volatile oils into a dish.
20
min prep
15
min cook
60
kcal/cup
Serves
4
cups
Ingredients
Method
1
Soak tamarind in 1 cup warm water for 10 minutes, then squeeze out the pulp and discard the fibres.
2
Coarsely crush black pepper and cumin in a mortar — cracked texture, not fine powder.
✦ Freshly cracked pepper is non-negotiable. Pre-ground won't give you the heat or fragrance.
3
Combine tamarind water, chopped tomatoes, turmeric, salt and 2 cups water. Boil and simmer 8–10 min until tomatoes are completely soft.
4
Add the crushed pepper-cumin and fresh coriander. Simmer 3 more minutes. The rasam must be thin and brothy — add water if too thick.
✦ Rasam should never coat a spoon. If it does, add more water.
5
Heat ghee until shimmering. Add mustard seeds — wait for them to pop. Add dry red chilli, curry leaves and a pinch of asafoetida. Pour immediately over the rasam.
6
Add a squeeze of lemon off the heat. Taste, adjust salt, and serve piping hot in cups or over steamed rice.
✦ Paati's Notes
Rasam is medicine first, food second. When someone is unwell, the first thing made is rasam. The pepper clears the chest, the tamarind cleanses, the curry leaves heal. Don't rush the tempering — the moment the mustard pops and the curry leaves hit the ghee is the whole point.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?

Watermelon · Spiced · Cooling
Watermelon
Shikanji
तरबूज शिकंजी
India's spiced lemonade gets a summer upgrade — watermelon juice, fresh lime, kala namak and roasted cumin. Cold, crimson, and completely unreasonable to have just one glass of.
☀️
Best in May – JulyPeak watermelon season. Make in bulk. Keep refrigerated. Refill constantly.
Beginner
You are learning spiced extraction — using black salt and cumin to deepen and amplify the natural sweetness of fruit.
10
min prep
0
cooking
65
kcal/glass
Serves
2
glasses
Ingredients
Method
1
Blend the watermelon chunks until smooth. Strain through a fine sieve — press the pulp through to extract every drop of juice.
2
Add fresh lime juice, sugar, kala namak and roasted cumin powder to the watermelon juice. Stir well until sugar dissolves.
✦ Kala namak is the secret here — it makes it taste more like watermelon, not less.
3
Taste and adjust — it should be sweet, tart, and have that earthy mineral edge from the black salt.
4
Fill glasses with ice. Pour the shikanji over. Top with a splash of cold water if it's too intense. Garnish with a mint sprig and lime wheel.
✦ For a fizzy version, top with chilled sparkling water instead.
✦ Nani's Notes
Watermelon is mostly water, so the flavour concentrates beautifully when you strain out the foam. Don't skip the cumin — it seems odd but it makes the drink taste deeply Indian and completely itself. Serve immediately; it separates if it sits too long.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸

Ginger Tea · Milky · Warming
Adrak Chai
अदरक चाय
Strong milky tea with freshly pounded ginger — the first thing made when it rains, when someone is unwell, when the morning feels heavy, or for no reason at all. The most made drink in any Indian home.
🌧️
Best in June – SeptemberMonsoon season. One cup every two hours. Non-negotiable.
Beginner
You are learning infusion ratios — how the balance of water, milk, tea and ginger changes the character of the same drink.
5
min prep
8
min cook
90
kcal/cup
Serves
2
cups
Ingredients
Method
1
Pound the fresh ginger in a mortar until it's bruised and juicy — not a paste, just cracked open. This is everything.
✦ Pound, don't grate. Pounded ginger releases differently — more fragrance, less sharpness.
2
Bring water to a rolling boil in a small saucepan. Add the pounded ginger and boil for 2 minutes — you want the ginger to properly infuse.
3
Add the tea leaves. Reduce heat and simmer for 1–2 minutes until the water turns a deep amber.
✦ Don't boil the leaves too long — it turns bitter. Amber, not black.
4
Add milk and sugar. Bring back to a gentle boil, watching carefully — it will rise quickly. Let it rise once, stir, let it rise again if you want it stronger.
5
Strain through a fine sieve into cups. Serve immediately, with a biscuit if you have one.
✦ Nani's Notes
The ratio of water to milk is personal — more milk makes it creamier and sweeter, more water makes it sharper. Everyone has their version. The ginger amount is also personal. Start with one inch and adjust. Some days you need more.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸
#GharKaGlass  #KhaanaHoToAana
Milk Tea · Strong · Mumbai
Cutting Chai
कटिंग चाय
Mumbai's half-glass of strong, sweet milky tea — served at street stalls since forever. "Cutting" means half, because you share the cost and the moment with whoever is standing next to you.
☕
Best All Year RoundMorning, evening, after an argument, before a meeting. There is never a wrong time for cutting chai.
Beginner
You are learning reduction and concentration — how simmering and reboiling intensifies flavour and body in a milky tea.
3
min prep
7
min cook
95
kcal/glass
Serves
4
half glasses
Ingredients
Method
1
Bring water to a rolling boil. Add pounded ginger and boil 1 minute.
2
Add tea leaves and reduce heat. Simmer 2 minutes until deep reddish-brown.
✦ Strong is the whole point. Don't be timid with the leaves.
3
Add milk and sugar. Bring to a brisk boil — watch it, it rises fast. Let it rise once, then lower the heat.
4
Strain into small glasses — half-filled. The glass should be too hot to hold comfortably. That is correct.
✦ The size of the glass is part of the experience. Use the smallest you have.
✦ Chaiwala's Notes
More tea, less water, enough milk to turn it the colour of terracotta. Sugar is non-negotiable; this is not a drink for subtlety. Serve in the smallest glasses you have.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸

Coconut · Lime · Coastal
Nariyal
Nimbu Pani
नारियल नींबू पानी
Fresh tender coconut water lifted with lime juice, a pinch of kala namak and mint. The gentlest, most hydrating drink of the Indian summer — tastes like standing near the sea.
☀️
Best in April – AugustPeak summer and coastal monsoon. Drink before the sun gets too high.
Beginner
You are learning light-handed balancing — adding just enough acid and salt to lift a delicate ingredient without overwhelming it.
5
min prep
0
cooking
55
kcal/glass
Serves
2
glasses
Ingredients
Method
1
Pour fresh coconut water into a jug. If using a whole tender coconut, strain through a fine sieve to remove any shell fragments.
2
Add fresh lime juice, kala namak and sugar if using. Stir gently — coconut water is delicate.
✦ Taste first before adding sugar. Good coconut water is already sweet.
3
Taste and balance — lime just audible beneath the coconut, black salt a whisper at the finish.
4
Pour over ice. Garnish with mint and a lime wedge. Serve immediately — coconut water loses its freshness quickly.
✦ Nani's Notes
The best version is from a fresh tender coconut hacked open in front of you. Packaged versions work but they are a different thing entirely. If you can find fresh coconuts, use them. The difference is not subtle.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★ ★ ★ ★ ★
What would you change next time?
Made this? Share it on Instagram 📸
#GharKaGlass  #KhaanaHoToAana
Black Tea · Spiced · Kerala
Sulaimani
Chai
سلیمانی چائے
A spiced black tea from the Malabar coast of Kerala — no milk, just whole spices, lime, and strong steeped tea. The tea of hospitality, of long conversations, of after-dinner quiet. Named after Sulaiman, a term of Arabic origin meaning the tea of peace.
❄️
Best in November – FebruaryHarsh winters and Kerala monsoons. Drunk after heavy meals. A digestive and a comfort in one cup.
Intermediate
You are learning spice infusion — how whole spices release differently from ground ones, and how steeping time controls intensity.
5
min prep
8
min cook
30
kcal/cup
Serves
2
cups
Ingredients
Method
1
Lightly crush the cardamom pods, cloves, and black pepper corns with a mortar. You want them cracked open, not powdered.
2
Bring water to a rolling boil. Add the crushed spices and the cinnamon stick. Boil for 3 minutes to let the spices bloom fully.
✦ Whole spices need real heat and time. Don't rush this step — it is where the flavour is built.
3
Add the tea leaves. Reduce heat and simmer for 2 minutes. The water should turn a deep, clear amber — not cloudy, not black.
✦ Sulaimani is a clear tea. Over-boiling the leaves makes it bitter and opaque. Watch the colour.
4
Strain through a fine sieve into cups. Add sugar and stir until dissolved.
5
Squeeze a wedge of lime into each cup — just before serving, not before. The lime must be fresh and bright, not cooked into the tea.
✦ The lime goes in last, off the heat. This keeps it sharp and fragrant.
✦ From Kerala
Sulaimani chai is served without milk because it was originally made for guests who kept long fasts — sailors, merchants, travellers on the Malabar coast. The spices aid digestion, the lime cuts through richness, and the black tea keeps you awake and warm. It is generous tea.
How did it turn out?
Rate it, jot what you'd change, make it yours.
★★ ★★ ★
